source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5510__Tract-Holding-Company-I-LLC/2025 1121 Tract Comments Re Large Loads Interconnection ANOPR_ RM26-4.txt
overall_summary: Tract, a data center site developer, supports the ANOPR goals and urges FERC to adopt a standardized large load interconnection process that applies cost-causation principles to deposits and financial security, mirrors generator interconnection queue management reforms, and ensures milestone-based security posting and assignability of agreements. The filing argues that overly burdensome or unpredictable requirements will deter investment and harm the U.S. data center industry's competitive position.

quotes.text: The rules that emerge from this proceeding should ensure that developers continue to be able to compete for load interconnection capacity without confronting unduly burdensome barriers to entry.
quotes.concern: Interconnection rules must not create barriers that disadvantage data center developers

quotes.text: any rules adopted by the Commission should provide an even playing field for all data center developers, without unduly discriminating against companies on the basis of their business model or size.
quotes.concern: Rules should apply equally regardless of developer size or business model

quotes.text: Tract supports DOE's proposal to study load and hybrid facilities together with generating facilities located at the same point of interconnection. Such joint study process should be at the option of both the load and generation customer and should be designed in such a way to result in faster and/or less costly interconnection for both the load and the generator.
quotes.concern: Joint study of load and generation should be optional and produce faster or cheaper interconnection

quotes.text: Tract agrees with adopting standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties, provided that they are consistent with the Commission's cost-causation principles.
quotes.concern: Deposits and penalties must align with cost-causation principles

quotes.text: Adopting deposits or penalties that are not sized to reflect a project's impact on the system will unnecessarily and economically deter data center development, threatening the ability to achieve the Administration's dual goals of energy dominance and winning the AI race.
quotes.concern: Over-sized deposits will deter data center investment

quotes.text: The Commission should establish a standardized methodology, whether by requiring fixed deposit amounts based on load size, by kWh, or through another metric, whereby customers can reasonably calculate anticipated charges and plan their investments accordingly.
quotes.concern: A standardized cost methodology is needed so customers can forecast charges

quotes.text: The Commission should apply the lessons it learned in the generator interconnection process and adopt similar deadlines for studying standardized load interconnection requests.
quotes.concern: Load interconnection should have mandatory study deadlines like generator interconnection

quotes.text: the Commission should establish standardized rules for each step in the large load interconnection process, the expected timing of each step, and other guidelines governing queue management, including whether the transmission provider is employing a cluster study or sequential study model, similar to generator interconnection.
quotes.concern: Queue management rules should be standardized and transparent like generator interconnection

quotes.text: Tract appreciates the need to provide financial security for load interconnection requests, but believes financial security requirements should be clear, predictable, in line with cost-causation, and tied to project progress.
quotes.concern: Financial security must be predictable, cost-causation-based, and milestone-linked

quotes.text: Consistent with the Commission's cost-causation principles, financial security should only be required for actual incremental costs caused by the large load customer, and should not provide a windfall to transmission providers in the event of default.
quotes.concern: Security must cover only actual incremental costs, not act as a revenue guarantee for utilities

quotes.text: Tract recommends the Commission require large load interconnection customers to only post financial security as they approach key project milestones (i.e., to support detailed design, permitting and right-of-way acquisition, procurement, pad acceptance, construction and energization), rather than up front as a lump sum because this places an unnecessary financial burden on the large load interconnection customer and creates incremental impediments to development.
quotes.concern: Security should be posted at project milestones, not as a lump sum upfront

quotes.text: The Commission should require that large load interconnection agreements and any related financial security agreements be assignable to creditworthy third parties.
quotes.concern: Interconnection agreements should be transferable to creditworthy third parties

quotes.text: Tract agrees that load and generation interconnection requests should have the option to undertake netted study situations, but it should not be required.
quotes.concern: Netted hybrid study should be optional, not mandatory

quotes.text: Tract agrees with the proposal to expedite the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable.
quotes.concern: Curtailable and dispatchable loads should receive expedited study

quotes.text: To the extent any network upgrades are assigned through interconnection studies to load or hybrid facilities, such costs should be offset through a crediting mechanism based on the actual usage of those upgrades by other networked customers.
quotes.concern: Load-assigned upgrade costs should be credited based on actual shared use by other customers

quotes.text: Tract agrees that there must be a plan to implement the proposed reforms that recognize the time, capital, and effort spent on requests that are already being studied for interconnection. Negatively affecting those existing requests not only wastes individual requestor's time, capital, and effort, but could also negatively impact all large load requests by causing cascading re-studies that delay or increase the costs of other large load requests.
quotes.concern: Transition plan must protect in-flight requests from re-study burdens

bins.name: Optional joint load-generation study
bins.description: Tract supports studying load and co-located generation together at the same point of interconnection. It insists this be at both parties' option and designed to produce faster or cheaper outcomes, not imposed as a requirement.

bins.name: Cost-causation deposits and security
bins.description: Tract accepts standardized deposits and financial security in principle but insists they be sized to actual incremental costs, not set as revenue guarantees for utilities. Security should be posted at project milestones rather than as an upfront lump sum, and agreements must be assignable to creditworthy third parties.

bins.name: Voluntary net-study for hybrid facilities
bins.description: Tract agrees hybrid facilities should have the option to elect a netted interconnection study but opposes making it mandatory, noting that some hybrids need full injection or withdrawal rights beyond what netting would allow.

bins.name: Expedited study for curtailable loads
bins.description: Tract endorses fast-tracking interconnection studies for loads that commit to curtailability or dispatchability, suggesting a 60-day study target using cluster or serialized queue processes.

bins.name: Credit mechanism for shared upgrade costs
bins.description: Tract supports crediting load customers for network upgrade costs when other customers use those upgrades, with credits continuing until 100% cost recovery rather than ending after a fixed period.

bins.name: Standardized study rules and deadlines
bins.description: Tract urges FERC to apply Order No. 2023-style mandatory study deadlines and queue management rules to large load interconnection, including disclosure of timelines, process steps, and how competing requests are handled.

bins.name: Cost-causation financial requirements
bins.description: Tract consistently argues that all financial requirements, deposits, security amounts, and upgrade cost assignments must follow cost-causation principles and align with the generator interconnection precedent. Over-sized charges will deter investment in data center development.

bins.name: Optional co-located generation study
bins.description: Tract supports optional joint study of co-located load and generation but opposes requiring it, arguing that flexibility in hybrid study elections is needed to accommodate diverse commercial arrangements.

bins.name: Flexibility incentives for curtailable loads
bins.description: Tract backs expedited study for loads offering curtailability or dispatchability as a tool to reduce system impact and speed interconnection.

bins.name: Usage-based upgrade cost credits
bins.description: Tract supports crediting load customers for network upgrade costs proportional to actual use by other networked customers, with no fixed expiration on the credit period.

bins.name: Even playing field for all developers
bins.description: Tract stresses that interconnection rules must not create barriers or discriminate based on developer size or business model, given that non-hyperscale operators own most U.S. data center capacity today and will continue to do so.

bins.name: Predictable cost methodology for planning
bins.description: Tract argues that a standardized, transparent cost methodology is necessary so large load customers can forecast charges and make informed investment decisions, reducing stranded-investment risk for all parties.

bins.name: Transparent queue management rules
bins.description: Tract calls for standardized queue rules that disclose timelines, process steps, and how multiple competing requests are managed, mirroring the cluster and sequential study options in generator interconnection.

bins.name: Milestone-based security posting
bins.description: Tract recommends that financial security be posted incrementally at key project milestones rather than as an upfront lump sum, matching each dollar of security to each dollar the transmission provider commits, no more and no earlier.

bins.name: Assignability of interconnection agreements
bins.description: Tract recommends that interconnection and financial security agreements be assignable by right to creditworthy third parties, affiliates, or successors to support project financing, continuity, and a competitive development ecosystem.

bins.name: Protect in-flight requests during transition
bins.description: Tract supports a transition plan that gives existing interconnection applicants the option to benefit from new rules without being required to re-study or bear added costs, avoiding cascading delays.
